title: Production possibility curves
summary:
## What a PPC shows

A **production possibility curve (PPC)** — also called a production possibility frontier (PPF) — shows the **maximum combinations of two goods** an economy can produce when all resources are **fully and efficiently employed**, with fixed technology.

Typically drawn with two goods (e.g. *capital goods* vs *consumer goods*) on the axes. The curve is the *boundary* of what is achievable.

## Key points on and off the curve

- **On the curve** → output is *productively efficient* and resources are *fully employed*.
- **Inside the curve** → resources are *unemployed or used inefficiently* (e.g. during a recession).
- **Outside the curve** → currently *unattainable* (impossible without growth).

## Opportunity cost on a PPC

Moving along the curve from one point to another means producing **more of one good and less of the other** — that sacrifice is the **opportunity cost**.

- A **straight-line PPC** → *constant* opportunity cost (resources equally suited to both goods).
- A **curved (concave to origin) PPC** → *increasing* opportunity cost, because resources are not equally suited to producing both goods. As you specialise in one good, you must give up ever-larger amounts of the other. This is the usual shape.

## Shifts of the PPC = economic growth

The whole curve shifts **outward** with **economic growth** — more or better resources or technology:
- increase in the **quantity** of factors (e.g. larger workforce, more capital from investment),
- increase in the **quality** of factors (education/training, better technology),
- discovery of new resources.
It shifts **inward** with a fall in resources (war, natural disaster, mass emigration). A shift in only one axis shows growth concentrated in one industry.

## Capital vs consumer goods (growth trade-off)

An economy choosing **more capital goods today** (point favouring investment) sacrifices current consumption but enjoys a **larger outward PPC shift in future** — investment drives long-run growth. Choosing mostly consumer goods raises living standards now but limits future growth.

## Actual vs potential output

- **Potential growth** = an outward shift of the PPC (capacity rises).
- **Actual growth** = moving from inside the curve *towards* it (using spare capacity) — this is the macro distinction between LRAS shifts and closing an output gap.

## Why it matters

The PPC is the simplest model of scarcity, choice, opportunity cost, efficiency, unemployment and growth — all on one diagram. It reappears throughout macro (potential vs actual output).

keyTerms.definition: A curve showing the maximum combinations of two goods producible with full, efficient use of resources.
Producing on the PPC, where it is impossible to make more of one good without making less of another.
The rising sacrifice of one good as more of another is produced, giving the PPC its concave shape.
An increase in productive capacity, shown by an outward shift of the PPC.
An outward shift of the PPC from more/better resources or technology.
A move from inside the PPC towards it as spare capacity is used up.

diagrams: **Basic PPC:** Good A on one axis, Good B on the other; concave (bowed-out) curve. Mark a point ON the curve (efficient), a point INSIDE (unemployment/inefficiency), and a point OUTSIDE (unattainable).
**Opportunity cost:** show a movement from one point on the curve to another, with arrows showing more of Good A and less of Good B.
**Growth:** show the whole curve shifting OUTWARD (PPC1 → PPC2) for economic growth, or inward for a fall in resources.
**Capital vs consumer goods:** axes = capital goods vs consumer goods; show that choosing more capital goods today leads to a larger future outward shift.
tips: Label axes with **two specific goods** and always mark points inside, on and outside the curve to show full understanding.
Explain the **concave shape** with *increasing opportunity cost* — resources are not equally suited to both goods. This is a common higher-mark point.
Distinguish a **movement along** the PPC (reallocating resources) from a **shift** (growth/decline) — same shift-vs-movement discipline as demand & supply.
Use the **capital vs consumer goods** trade-off to discuss investment and future growth — great for evaluation.
Link a point **inside** the PPC to **unemployment / a negative output gap** for synoptic macro marks.
